turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Number 88-1916, Minnesota v. Robert Darren Olson.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case presents two critical and recurring issues under the Fourth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. First, when does an overnight visitor have a sufficient expectation of privacy in a house to enable him to challenge his arrest there? The state contends that merely hiding overnight in a place with permission of the owners is not enough to create a privacy expectation. And secondly, is flight of a dangerous felon believed to be armed an exigent circumstance which justifies a warrantless arrest? The state contends that it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what's at issue here is a statement that was given at the police station?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: At the time that he was at the police station, was there not probable cause to hold the prisoner?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then, how is it that this statement is really related to what occurred in the dwelling in any event?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, the respondent is claiming that his arrest was illegal under the Fourth Amendment and that his statement was a fruit of the illegal arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there would have been a right, I take it, to let him go for 30 seconds and re-arrest him after he got outside of the dwelling.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I suppose that's true, Your Honor. The state, however, has never —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There's no right to be free from custody once they are in the station house just because there has been an unlawful entry in the dwelling, is there?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no right to an immediate release at the station house, a release from custody, merely because there has been an unlawful entry into the dwelling, even assuming the entry to the dwelling was unlawful?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I suppose that's true, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, why is it that we really have to reach this issue? Why isn't this just an admissible statement?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, the state, of course, has always felt that this is an admissible statement because the arrest was legal, and we've never really argued --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but you don't — but you don't -- you don't argue the point that the statement's not tainted in any event.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: No. It was never argued below and we've never argued it through the process, Your Honor. It's also the state's position that this Court would not need to reach the exigent circumstances issue if it ruled that the respondent did not have privacy interest in the home, also, and that's been the state's main contention all along. Respondent was a getaway driver involved with his co-defendant in an armed robbery of a gas station and the cold-blooded murder of the gas station attendant. Was respondent's temporary hideout his home? It's clear that a person has a constitutionally protected right to privacy in one's own home. It's also clear that one can have a constitutionally protected right to privacy in a place other than one's home under certain circumstances. This case is about when. This Court has stated —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, doesn't an ordinary house guest have some expectation that he won't be bothered by strangers while within the home? Isn't there some reasonable expectation there, when I'm a guest in someone else's home?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I think there's some expectation, although whether it's —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And isn't it a reasonable one, one that society is prepared to accept?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, I think it depends on all the circumstances of the case. Certainly, if a visitor — for instance a relative, if I stay in my parents' home for a period of time, I would have an expectation of privacy in that home. However, if I were just a casual visitor to the home, my expectation of privacy would be much less.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, don't we need some fairly clear rules for the police, things that aren't so fact specific? How's the policeman going to know the length of the visit, or whether a key has been given, or who has given permission? I mean, why shouldn't we try to craft and follow fairly simple rules to govern the police action?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I agree with you that a simple rule would be nice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you're certainly not asking us to craft one here. It's very fact-specific and very complex.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, I have two responses to that. One is that I think that we probably don't want the policeman to be making standing decisions, that in fact what we would want in the society is for policemen to be concerned with public safety issues and —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why, here, shouldn't the police go get a warrant?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: The police in this case didn't get a warrant because they felt that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because it was Sunday. It was a "never on Sunday" rule, apparently.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I think if this case had occurred on the weekday that they would have done the same thing. They were faced with a murderer, someone who they felt was dangerous, someone who they felt was armed --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's the exigent circumstances. But just on -- on the right of the police to enter the dwelling, Justice O'Connor indicates that the rule you argue is fact specific, and I might add it's all post hoc. You don't know about the key, and how long he's been there, and whether he has the right to exclude guests until after he is arrested and it comes out in suppression, so it's a very unworkable rule, it seems to me.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, presumptively, if the police are entering the home they want them to get a warrant, that's right, but if —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Peek, your answer is, it doesn't matter, does it? The police are breaking the law. The only thing we're arguing about -- there's no doubt that they violated the Constitution. The question we are arguing about today is, have they violated your right as well as his right? It's clear that they're violating the right of the person who owns the premises. The only question is, whether they, in addition, are violating any right of the guest, isn't that right?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So predictability has nothing to do with this. The — the police don't — the police are in the wrong no matter what, isn't that right?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, presumptively they should get a warrant if they're going into a private home. Later —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They have clearly violated somebody's constitutional right. The only thing we're arguing about is whether they've violated the guests as well as what they clearly have violated — the owners.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, that is -- that is the crux of the issue in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So police predictability is really out of the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, except for the fact that they know this man isn't the owner, and they don't care if they violate the owner's constitutional rights because they're not worried about introducing any evidence against him. They don't even care about this — the guy they're looking for.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, I don't think they felt that they were violating anyone's rights because they felt they had exigent circumstances to make the warrant --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, forgetting exigent circumstances for the moment, is it not your position that the police can arrest a person in a dwelling if that person has no reasonable expectation of privacy there?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's your position, isn't it?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: No, no. What we've been saying all along is that -- that we want the policemen to concern themselves not with standing but with probable cause and with exigent circumstances and the other things that they need to decide, and that they should try to proceed legally based on those facts. Later, a judge can decide whether or not they were right, and whether or not whose rights were --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If they were right, was there not -- did they not have the authority to enter the home and make the arrest, or do you concede that they were violating the rights of the owner of this home?

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: Well, the state has never conceded. It hasn't been an issue, because there's been no evidence against the owners and the owners haven't raised it. The issue all along has been the defendant's rights, and so the state has never conceded that the arrest was illegal, but if the arrest was illegal, the issue is, whose rights were violated? I think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If there were exigent circumstances, the arrest was not illegal.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. I think our position has been that societal -- the societal -- public policy would indicate that we do not want policemen to make arrests based -- to make entries based on whether or not someone has standing. We want them to presumptively get a warrant if they need to, and if they don't need to they go in, and later they find out who's in the house and who has interest and later the court can sort out who has standing to object to whatever happens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Quite so, and in -- in a way a vague and unpredictable rule is even better, right, isn't it, because then they can never be sure that even though they're violating the Constitution there will be no harmful consequence following from it? We should really have a very, very unpredictable rule.

turns.name: ANNE E. PEEK
turns.text: I don't think -- I think the problem is it's very difficult to -- to formulate a simple rule in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We'll resume there at 1:00.
